chunk_summary: Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) is a chronic subclinical intestinal condition linked to stunting and undernutrition, driven by repeated fecal-oral pathogen exposure. Clinical focus includes improving diet, WASH behaviors, and early diarrhea treatment while avoiding non-standardized tests in routine practice.
learning_objectives: Understand the mechanisms of environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) and its impact on stunting and chronic undernutrition.
Identify actionable interventions, including dietary improvements, WASH behaviors, and early diarrhea management, to mitigate EED effects.
Recognize when to pursue targeted evaluations for alternative diagnoses instead of attributing all symptoms to EED.
key_facts: EED involves gut inflammation, increased intestinal permeability, and systemic inflammation, impairing growth pathways like IGF-1 activity.
Non-standardized tests like stool inflammatory markers and lactulose:mannitol permeability tests are not recommended for routine clinical use.
Interventions include improving diet quality, ensuring protein adequacy, prioritizing WASH behaviors, and treating diarrhea early with ORS and zinc.
Persistent diarrhea or systemic symptoms should prompt evaluation for conditions like coeliac disease, giardiasis, TB, or inflammatory bowel disease.
Growth monitoring should focus on illness frequency and growth velocity over 8–12 months.
reference_quote: The WASH Benefits and SHINE trials: interpretation and implications for policy and practice (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27567645/).
document_key_topics: Tests to avoid in routine practice
Interventions for stunting and chronic undernutrition
